column1: # Principle: Number arithmetic — Integer + Decimal with contagion.
# AQL has TWO concrete numeric types under the same Number root:
#   Scalar/Number/Integer  — written as digits with optional leading minus
#   Scalar/Number/Decimal  — written with a decimal point
# The math primitives (add, sub, mul, negate, abs) are sigged on
# [TNumber, TNumber] (or [TNumber|] for unary — the `|` makes the arg
# forward-eligible with stack fallback). Integer and Decimal both
# match Number. Each handler dispatches on argument types at runtime:
#     - both args Integer  →  integer op,  result is Integer
#     - any arg Decimal    →  float64 op,  result is Decimal  (contagion)
# This is the "decimal contagion" rule, mirroring the production
# 1. Decimal literals round-trip with a guaranteed decimal point.
#    keeping Decimal visually distinct from Integer in user output.
# 2. add/sub/mul accept any combination of Integer and Decimal args.
#    No coercion error; the type lattice's Number parent unifies both.
# 3. Result type is Integer iff both args are Integer; otherwise
#    Decimal. This is observable in the rendered form (Integer renders
#    without a decimal point; Decimal always with one).
# 4. Mirror equivalence (mirror.tsv) and swap form behave the same way
#    across mixed-type calls — the type promotion does not interact
# 5. negate is a unary [TNumber|] op (sig "[Number|]" — barrier at
#    position 1, the single arg is forward-eligible with stack fallback).
#    Same Integer/Decimal dispatch as the binary ops.
# 6. Float64 imprecision is preserved verbatim. `0.1 add 0.2` produces
#    `0.30000000000000004`, not `0.3`. The spec runner does not
#    silently round; see SPEC_REPORT.md §2 for the apd-port plan
# 7. Type mismatches still error: a String, Boolean, atom, or list in
#    a Number slot fails sig-match with `signature_error`.
# The Integer/Decimal split is the foundational lattice decision for
# the type system. The contagion rule (any Decimal taints the result)
# ensures arithmetic is closed: numeric ops never silently lose
# precision by truncating a Decimal back to Integer, and they never
# refuse a mixed call. Both properties together let user code mix
# For each of {add, sub, mul} we exercise all four type-combinations:
#   ID — Integer + Decimal  (contagion via second arg)
#   DI — Decimal + Integer  (contagion via first arg)
# For sub we additionally cover the swap form (`a sub b`) for each
# combination, since sub is the engine's canonical non-commutative
# For negate we cover both Integer and Decimal inputs in both
# forms — `negate X` (forward) and `X negate` (via stack fallback).
# Handlers compute `args[0] op args[1]` (so `sub 10 3` is the
# canonical mirror form returning 10-3=7; `10 sub 3` is the swap
# form returning 3-10=-7). The Decimal payload is float64; the
# renderer is `formatDecimal` which appends ".0" when there's no

column3: formatDecimal preserves the decimal point on whole-valued floats
whole-valued decimal renders as "2.0" — keeps type tag visible
Integer + Decimal coexist; type tag visible per-value

column3: II — both Integer; result is Integer (no decimal point)
ID — Integer + Decimal; contagion → Decimal result
DI — Decimal + Integer; contagion → Decimal result
DD — both Decimal; result is Decimal
stack=[2, 3.0]: top=3.0 → sig[0], deeper=2 → sig[1]; 3.0+2=5.0 (DI under handler view; D contagion)

column1: # sub is non-commutative. Handler convention (mirror.tsv): result =

# sig from forward tokens (source order), then stack (top down).
# So the natural infix reading `10 sub 3` produces sig=[3, 10] and
# `sub 3 10` (prefix) belong to the same equivalence class.
# `sub 10 3`, `3 sub 10`, `3 10 sub` are the OTHER class with
# Each row exercises one of these arrangements across all four
# Class returning 10 - X (= 7 with X=3, in II):

# A specific decimal-only subtraction surfaces a float artefact.
# Per the handler convention `b - a`, the form `sub a b` returns

zero × decimal → zero, but Decimal-typed via contagion

column1: # Sig [Number|] — barrier at position 1 (= N for unary). The single
# arg is forward-eligible with stack fallback, so both forms work:
#   X negate   — fwd-scan empty, stack supplies the arg
# Integer in, Integer out; Decimal in, Decimal out.

column3: I forward: forward dispatch on Integer
D forward: forward dispatch on Decimal — preserves Decimal type
negate Decimal zero → -0.0 (IEEE sign flip; +0.0 and -0.0 are distinct bit patterns)
negate fwd-scans "residue" (String), fails Number — break at fwd=1; consumes the 5.5 it already collected; "residue" stays
composes via paren: inner add = 5; negate(5) = -5
bare nested forward — function-word boundary, see nested-forward.tsv

column1: # abs is forward-prec [TNumber]. Integer in → Integer out;
# Decimal in → Decimal out. Uses math.Abs for IEEE 754 correctness:

column3: D positive: identity (renders with .0 via formatDecimal)
D negative zero — math.Abs clears the sign bit (NOT "-0.0")
bare nested forward — same boundary rule as elsewhere

column1: # IEEE 754 binary float cannot represent 0.1 or 0.2 exactly. Their
# sum is 0.30000000000000004, not 0.3. aqleng preserves this verbatim
# — no display rounding, no silent truncation. See SPEC_REPORT.md §2
# for the exact-decimal-port plan that would change this row.

column3: same value via the mirror form

column1: # Composition with parens (parens force the inner to evaluate before
# the outer's slot is filled — same nesting rule as integers).
